title: Today's AI Brief: Google's Dreambeans
summary: Google's new AI tool, Dreambeans, turns life into a cartoon. AI's empty promise and more.
imageAlt: Image from source article for Google’s Dreambeans, its weirdest-named AI tool to date, will turn your life into a cartoon
imageCaption: Google’s Dreambeans, its weirdest-named AI tool to date, will turn your life into a cartoon
imageSourceTitle: Google’s Dreambeans, its weirdest-named AI tool to date, will turn your life into a cartoon

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to Today's AI Brief. I am looking at some fresh signals from the world of artificial intelligence. Google has just launched a new AI-fueled app called Dreambeans, and I have to say, it's got a weirdly catchy name. But what's really interesting is what it does - and I'm excited to dive into that.

Dreambeans is an app for iOS and Android that uses AI to literally animate your life. Imagine your daily routine turned into a cartoon - that's what Dreambeans promises. According to TechCrunch, Google Labs, the tech giant's team devoted to experimental product design, is behind this new app. I'm curious to see how this will be adopted, and what kind of creative possibilities it will open up. For instance, will people use it to make their mundane tasks look fun and exciting, or will it become a new form of artistic expression?

Google’s Dreambeans, its weirdest-named AI tool to date, will turn your life into a cartoon is also worth a quick mention. The main content says: Google Labs, the tech giant’s team devoted to experimental product design, has launched a new AI-fueled app for iOS and Android that will quite literally animate your life. Behold, Dreambeans . Why is it called that? We’ll get to that later. First, what is it? Gozde Oznur, the product lead behind the new app, told TechCrunch that the idea is to use data cul... The point is not to force it into a giant theory; it is simply part of today's news mix.

As AI gets better, it reveals an empty promise, from theverge.com, is also worth a quick mention. My quick read is that today's set of stories is useful because it gives us texture, not a sermon. Each item has its own little job: one tells us what changed, another tells us what people are worried about, and another gives the day its mood.
